tossups.question: Ousmane Sembène’s (“sem-BEN’s”) mentor Mark Donskoy directed a trilogy about this author while working for the studio that now bears his name, which produced films like I am Twenty. A play by this author indirectly inspired Neecha Nagar, the only Indian film to win a Cannes Grand Prix. After watching a Lumière brothers film at a brothel, this author used the pseudonym “I. M. Pacatus” to write a review that begins, “last night, I was in the kingdom of the shadows.” This author created an old woman-turned-radical who titles the 1926 magnum opus of montage theorist Vsevolod (*) Pudovkin, Mother. This author’s surname appears in the title of a film in which William Hurt played policeman Arkady Renko, a creation of novelist Martin Cruz Smith. The Criterion Collection sells a joint release of Jean Renoir and Akira Kurosawa’s adaptations of a play by this author about the tenants of a run-down house. For 10 points, name this playwright of The Lower Depths.
tossups.question_sanitized: Ousmane Sembene's ("sem-BEN's") mentor Mark Donskoy directed a trilogy about this author while working for the studio that now bears his name, which produced films like I am Twenty. Pacatus" to write a review that begins, "last night, I was in the kingdom of the shadows." This author created an old woman-turned-radical who titles the 1926 magnum opus of montage theorist Vsevolod (*) Pudovkin, Mother. This author's surname appears in the title of a film in which William Hurt played policeman Arkady Renko, a creation of novelist Martin Cruz Smith. The Criterion Collection sells a joint release of Jean Renoir and Akira Kurosawa's adaptations of a play by this author about the tenants of a run-down house. For 10 points, name this playwright of The Lower Depths.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Maxim Gorky [accept Gorky Film Studios; accept Gorky Park]

tossups.question: One of these figures partly titles a novel in which Zakeya (zah-KAY-ah) kills a corrupt mayor on the banks of the Nile, by Nawal El Saadawi. Samba N'Doulougou (n'DOO-loo-goo) serves as a "walking newspaper" in a novel partly titled for one of these figures, in which women march from the city of Thiès (CHESS) after Bakayoko leads a railway strike. An Ousmane Sembène (OOS-mahn sem-BEN) novel is titled for one of these figures' Bits of Wood. One of these figures partly titles a novel in which John Goodcountry witnesses a famine caused by colonial administrator T. K. Winterbottom imprisoning the priest Ezeulu, which concludes its author's African Trilogy. One of these figures titles a novel about the incestuous fraternal twins Rahul and Estha. For 10 points, name these figures who title a Chinua Achebe novel about an Arrow of one of them and an Arundhati Roy novel about one of Small Things.
tossups.answer: gods [or dieu; or alá; or Allah; accept God Dies by the Nile or God's Bits of Wood or Arrow of God or The God of Small Things or Mawt al-rajul al-waḥīd ʻalá al-arḍ or Les bouts de bois de Dieu]

tossups.question: At the end of this director’s breakthrough film, Cream’s “Steppin’ Out” plays on the radio before a car containing Amy Robinson’s Teresa crashes into a fire hydrant. Films such as Ousmane Sembène’s (“oose-mahn som-BEN’s”) Black Girl have been restored by this director’s World Cinema Project. One of several of this director’s films edited by Thelma Schoonmaker has opening credits that are accompanied by (*) Mascagni’s “Cavalleria Rusticana: Intermezzo.” The protagonist of that 1980 film looks into a mirror as he quotes Brando’s “I coulda been a contender” monologue from Elia Kazan’s On the Waterfront. This man directed Robert De Niro playing the boxer Jake LaMotta. For 10 points, name this American director of Mean Streets and Raging Bull.

tossups.question: A 2019 film from this country, which concerns the graves of unpaid construction workers being dug by their employer so their spirits can rest, was the first film directed by a black woman to compete for the Palme d’Or. A director from this country adapted his own novel into a film about a businessman who suffers erectile dysfunction on his third wedding night. In a 1973 film from this country, a cowherd and his girlfriend decide to ride away from their village on a motorcycle mounted with a bull’s skull. Films from this country include (*) Atlantics, Touki Bouki, and Xala. A maid from this country gifts a mask to the exploitative French couple who hire her in the 1966 film Black Girl. For 10 points, name this African country, home to Mati Diop, Diop Djibril Mambéty, and Ousmane Sembène, with a film industry based in Dakar.

tossups.question: A film from this country pairs tracking shots of children running alongside a car and shots from behind of a naked man shouting and standing in a convertible. That film shows shots of a hand grabbing a decoration on a motorcycle while the two main characters have sex off screen. A film made by a director from this country plays opening credits over a long shot of a large white ocean liner docking, before a sequence shot from the POV of a passenger driving through a port city. The first black woman to show a film at Cannes is for a film set in this country; that film, (*) Atlantics, follows a woman whose boyfriend has gone missing after taking a boat to Spain. A director from this country adapted his own novels and stories into films like La Noire de and The Money-Order and was the first person from sub-Saharan Africa to direct a feature film and is often called the father of African cinema. A couple tries to take a boat from Paris to leave this country in Touki Bouki. For 10 points, identify this African country, home of Djibril Diop Mambéty, Mati Diop, and the director of Ceddo and Xala, Ousmane Sembène.

tossups.question: A poet who spent most of his life in this country addresses “strange men who were not men” in a poem that compares Europeans to the title “Vultures” in the collection Pounding. One author from this country wrote a novel formatted as a book-length letter written by the protagonist to her sister after her husband's death. That author from this country is Mariama Ba. A poet from this country wrote the collections Shadow Songs and Nocturnes, and wrote about a woman “clothed with your colour which is life” in his poem Black Woman. One author from this country wrote about Bakayoko giving an impassioned speech against overseers during a railway strike. Another author from this main setting of God's Bits of Wood by Ousmane Sembene collaborated with Aime Cesaire to found the Negritude movement, before becoming this country's first president. For ten points, name this home country of Leopold Senghor.

tossups.question: Note. Description acceptable. In a Senegalese novel, the businessman El Hadji is cursed with this handicap after he cheats a beggar. That novel, Xala, is titled for this condition, and was adapted into a film directed by its author, Ousmane Sembène. In John Pepper Clark-Bekederemo’s Song of a Goat, Zifa suffers from this handicap. In the decadent novel À Rebours, the protagonist throws a black-themed dinner to celebrate this disability. In William Wycherley’s The Country Wife, Horner pretends to have this disability. In Medea, the title character is promised Aegeus’s protection after she pledges to help (*) him with this condition. This condition plagues the protagonist of a novel who loves Lady Brett Ashley. For 10 points, what condition is suffered by Jake Barnes, the protagonist of The Sun Also Rises, which leaves him sexually frustrated?
tossups.answer: impotence [accept answers like “infertility” or “childlessness” only after “Medea” is read]

tossups.question: Norah leaves one of these two countries to visit her father, who owns a holiday village in the other, in a novel in which Rudy drags his wife Fanta from one of these countries to the other. In another novel, a student at the Glowing Hearth school in one of these countries is sent to school in the other country at the behest of a relative known as the Most Royal Lady. The novels Three Strong Women and (*) Ambiguous Adventure are set in these two countries. Managers from one of these countries clash with workers from the other country who call them toubabs in a novel about a railroad strike, God’s Bits of Wood. For 10 points, name these two countries, one of which is the home country of Ousmane Sembène, and the other one of which is the European country which colonized it.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about women’s activism in West Africa, for 10 points each.
parts: Stella Obasanjo and Efua Dorkenoo campaigned against women undergoing this practice, which Thomas Sankara banned. Ousmane Sembène’s film Moolaadé criticized this surgery, which Facing Mount Kenya defended as a Kikuyu (“gih-KOO-yoo”) tradition.

Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti of the Abeokuta Women’s Union earned this title that was given to “warrant” officials who were “sat on” in the 1929 Igbo (“EE-boh”) “Women’s War.” Rulers like Charles Atangana and Moshoeshoe I (“mu-SHWAY-shway the first”) were styled as “paramount” ones.

In 1977, women protested this country’s “economic police” in Marché Madina. The historian Jean Suret-Canale taught in this bauxite-exporting country, the only former colony to vote for full independence in a 1958 referendum.
answers: female genital mutilation [or FGM/C; or female circumcision; accept genital cutting or gishiri cutting; accept infibulation or clitoridectomy or descriptions thereof]
chiefs [accept chiefdoms or chieftains or chieftainships; accept paramount chiefs or Oberhäuptlinge; accept warrant chiefs; accept Oloye]

Guinea [or Guinea-Conakry; or Republic of Guinea or République de Guinée; accept French Guinea or Guinée française; reject “Guinea-Bissau” or “Equatorial Guinea” or “French Guiana” or “Guyana” or “New Guinea”]

chiefs [accept chiefdoms or chieftains or chieftainships; accept paramount chiefs or Oberhauptlinge; accept warrant chiefs; accept Oloye]

bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about women's activism in West Africa, for 10 points each.
parts_sanitized: Stella Obasanjo and Efua Dorkenoo campaigned against women undergoing this practice, which Thomas Sankara banned. Ousmane Sembene's film Moolaade criticized this surgery, which Facing Mount Kenya defended as a Kikuyu ("gih-KOO-yoo") tradition.

Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti of the Abeokuta Women's Union earned this title that was given to "warrant" officials who were "sat on" in the 1929 Igbo ("EE-boh") "Women's War." Rulers like Charles Atangana and Moshoeshoe I ("mu-SHWAY-shway the first") were styled as "paramount" ones.

In 1977, women protested this country's "economic police" in Marche Madina. The historian Jean Suret-Canale taught in this bauxite-exporting country, the only former colony to vote for full independence in a 1958 referendum.

bonuses.leadin: A subculture centered on customized vehicles of this sort is the subject of Mitsuo Yanagimachi’s film God Speed You! Black Emperor. For 10 points each:
parts: Peter Fonda uses an American flag-patterned vehicle of what type in Dennis Hopper’s film Easy Rider?

The taxi driver “Blacksta” uses a colorful motorcycle emblazoned with a literal black star in this 2018 film by Wanuri Kahiu, which was banned in her home country of Kenya for depicting the lesbian romance of Kena and Ziki.

Beyoncé and Jay-Z repopularized a film from this country, Touki-Bouki, by featuring its image of a motorbike decorated with a cow’s skull in a 2018 photoshoot. The director of Black Girl, Ousmane Sembène, was from here.
answers: Senegal (Touki-Bouki was directed by Djibril Diop Mambéty.)
answers_sanitized: motorcycles [or motorbikes; accept choppers or hogs; prompt on bikes or cycles]
Rafiki
Senegal (Touki-Bouki was directed by Djibril Diop Mambety.)

parts_sanitized: Peter Fonda uses an American flag-patterned vehicle of what type in Dennis Hopper's film Easy Rider?

The taxi driver "Blacksta" uses a colorful motorcycle emblazoned with a literal black star in this 2018 film by Wanuri Kahiu, which was banned in her home country of Kenya for depicting the lesbian romance of Kena and Ziki.

Beyonce and Jay-Z repopularized a film from this country, Touki-Bouki, by featuring its image of a motorbike decorated with a cow's skull in a 2018 photoshoot. The director of Black Girl, Ousmane Sembene, was from here.

bonuses.leadin: Ousmane Sembène (“oos-MAWN sawm-BEN”) was born in this region’s capital of Ziguinchor (“zee-geen-SHORE”) and depicted it in his novel O Pays, mon beau people! (“oh pay, mon bo pee-PULL”). For 10 points each:
parts: Name this region of southern Senegal whose large Jola ethnic group have frequently sought independence from the Senegalese government.
The Casamance region is separated from the northern sections of Senegal by this small African country whose capital is Banjul.
Casamance separatists were involved in fomenting civil war in this southern neighbour of Senegal. This coastal country is one of the world’s most prominent narco-states.
answers_sanitized: Casamance
Gambia
Guinea-Bissau

bonuses.leadin: Trains have been a frequent presence in African literature. For 10 points each:
parts: Ousmane Sembene, a director and novelist from this country described a colonial railroad strike of workers in this country and an eastern neighbour in God’s Bits of Wood. Mariama Ba’s So Long a Letter is also set in this country.
Fiston Mwanza Mujila described the mining-town bar Tram 83 in his debut novel set in this country, which is the subject of Michela Wrong's In the Footsteps of Mr Kurtz.
The anonymous protagonist of this Ghanaian author’s The Beautyful Ones Are Not Yet Born works in a railway station and rails against the corruption of Kwame Nkrumah’s government.
answers_sanitized: Senegal
Democratic Republic of Congo (accept Belgian Congo)
Ayi Kwei Armah

bonuses.leadin: The Caine Prize is a yearly accolade awarded to an outstanding short story author from this continent. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this continent, whose postcolonial authors include Ayi Kwei Armah, Tayib Salih, and Nuruddin Farah.
This Kenyan author’s English-language novels include A Grain of Wheat and Petals of Blood, whereas novels written in his native Gikuyu include Wizard of the Crow.
This West African country’s literary tradition includes So Long a Letter, written by Mariama Ba, and God’s Bits of Wood, written by author and director Ousmane Sembene.
answers_sanitized: Africa
Ngugi wa Thiong'o
Republic of Senegal [or Republique du Senegal]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The Caine Prize is a yearly accolade awarded to an outstanding short story author from this continent. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Identify this continent, whose postcolonial authors include Ayi Kwei Armah, Tayib Salih, and Nuruddin Farah.

This West African country's literary tradition includes So Long a Letter, written by Mariama Ba, and God's Bits of Wood, written by author and director Ousmane Sembene.

bonuses.leadin: Collé, the second wife of a tribal council elder, ties a string of yarn across the doorstep of her house to protect four girls from this practice in the film Moolaadé. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this inhumane practice that has not been performed on a bilakoro. It often involves removal of the clitoral hood and glans.

Moolaadé was directed by this French-speaking African filmmaker and writer, whose first feature film, Black Girl, brought African cinema to international attention. He critiqued the corruption of government through the lens of a businessman with erectile dysfunction in Xala.
Ousmane Sembène hails from this West African country. Sembène was a member of this country's Serer ethnic group, like its first president, the poet Léopold Senghor.
answers_sanitized: female genital mutilation/cutting [or fgm/c; or female circumcision; or cutting]
Ousmane Sembene
Senegal

bonuses.leadin: This author wrote about the Muslim businessman El Hadji Kader Beye who is unable to consummate his marriage to his third wife N'Gone, and believes he's been cursed with the title impotence, in the novel Xala. FTPE:
parts: Name this author who wrote about a railroad strike in his home country in God's Bits of Wood.
That home country of Ousmane Sembene is this country, whose presedent Leopold Senghor wrote about ashes feeding the roots of life in his poem "Black Woman."
Another great Senegalese novel is this one by Mariama Bâ, which consists of correspondence between Ramatoulaye and Aissatou upon the death of Ramatoulaye’s husband.
answers: So Long a Letter [or Une si longue letter]
answers_sanitized: Ousmane Sembene
Senegal
So Long a Letter [or Une si longue letter]
